skill.name: computer-use-agents
skill.description: Build AI agents that interact with computers through screenshots, cursor movement, clicks, keyboard input, and shell-capable tool loops. The skill covers Anthropic Computer Use, OpenAI Operator and CUA concepts, open-source approaches, sandboxing, security controls, and vision-based GUI automation challenges.
skill.summary: Design safer computer-use agents with screen observation, desktop actions, and sandboxed execution patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static findings are largely documentation examples rather than executable package code, and no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found. The real risk is medium because the skill teaches screenshot capture, GUI control, bash-capable tool wiring, subprocess use, and temporary file handling for computer-use agents. Several weak-crypto, Windows SAM, and role-token alerts are false positives caused by ordinary text such as Claude model names, filesystem guidance, and Docker examples.

medium_findings.title: Computer Control Examples Require Isolation
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for screenshot capture, mouse clicks, keyboard input, scrolling, and model-directed actions. This is legitimate for computer-use agents, but it can affect the host desktop if implemented outside a sandbox.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line ranges directly show screen observation and desktop action patterns. The file also repeatedly recommends sandboxing, so this is a controlled but real misuse risk rather than confirmed malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Capable Tool Pattern
medium_findings.description: The Anthropic tool example defines a bash tool and routes tool calls to a bash handler. This is expected for the topic, but it can become command execution risk if copied without strict allowlists and sandbox boundaries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bash tool and dispatch path are explicit in the documented example. The handler body is truncated, so the exact command validation behavior is not shown.

medium_findings.title: Screenshot File Written To Temporary Storage
medium_findings.description: The example uses subprocess.run to call scrot and writes a screenshot to /tmp before reading it. This is a normal desktop automation pattern, but screenshots can contain sensitive information.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary screenshot path and subprocess call are shown directly. The surrounding text frames this as an implementation pattern, not exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts map to documentation text, model names, Docker snippets, or markdown structure. No evidence found of cryptographic code, hashing, encryption, or insecure cipher selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited ranges contain prose and setup examples, not cryptographic operations. No evidence found of MD5, DES, RC4, SHA1, or similar algorithm usage in executable logic.

low_findings.title: Static Windows SAM And Role-Token Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and LLM role-token alerts point to filesystem isolation guidance, not credential dumping or prompt injection. No text claims system authority, asks the evaluator to ignore instructions, or changes audit rules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The location discusses NETWORK, FILESYSTEM, CREDENTIALS, SYSCALLS, and RESOURCES as sandbox requirements. It is defensive guidance and contains no prompt override language.

low_findings.title: Docker And Filesystem Examples Are Defensive
low_findings.description: The hidden file, temp directory, and Unix shell detections occur in a Docker sandbox example that creates a non-root user, uses scoped temporary storage, and sets a read-only root filesystem.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected file and shell patterns are part of container setup and isolation guidance. They do not request access to host credentials or sensitive host paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Screen Capture And GUI Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples capture screenshots and automate clicks, typing, keypresses, and scrolling. These capabilities need explicit consent, logging, and sandboxing when implemented.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The automation operations are directly visible in the example code. The risk depends on deployment context, so it is dangerous as a pattern but not malicious by itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess-Based Screenshot Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The example invokes an external screenshot command and stores output in a temporary file. Implementers should restrict commands and protect screenshot contents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run call and temporary file path are explicit. The arguments are static, which lowers command injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Exposure
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented tool list includes a bash tool and dispatch logic for bash actions. This pattern requires sandboxing, command policy, and human review for sensitive operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly exposes a bash tool interface. The file does not show the full bash handler, so the final enforcement quality cannot be verified.

content.value_statement: Computer-use agents can misclick, expose screen data, or run unsafe actions when built without guardrails. This skill gives practical patterns for screen observation, action loops, tool wiring, and sandboxed execution.
actual_capabilities: Explains the perception, reasoning, action, and feedback loop for GUI agents.
Shows how screenshots, clicks, typing, keypresses, and scrolling fit into an agent loop.
Describes Docker-based isolation with restricted networks, scoped storage, and resource limits.
Outlines Anthropic Computer Use tool concepts for computer, bash, and text editor actions.
Highlights operational risks such as sensitive screenshots, runaway loops, and difficult UI controls.
limitations: Contains partial code examples that require completion before use.
Does not provide a complete production-ready computer-use agent.
Requires external model APIs, desktop tools, containers, and security review.
Some model and tool version references may need verification before implementation.

use_cases.title: Prototype A Desktop Agent
use_cases.description: Use the action-loop pattern to design an agent that observes a virtual desktop and performs simple GUI tasks.

use_cases.title: Plan A Secure Sandbox
use_cases.description: Apply the container isolation checklist before allowing an agent to view screens or execute commands.

use_cases.title: Review Computer-Use Risk
use_cases.description: Use the sharp edges and sandbox guidance to evaluate whether a GUI automation workflow is safe to deploy.

prompt_templates.title: Explain The Agent Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the perception, reasoning, action, and feedback loop for a computer-use agent. Keep the explanation practical and security aware.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Sandbox
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Docker-based sandbox for a computer-use agent. Include network, filesystem, credential, syscall, and resource controls.

prompt_templates.title: Map Tools To Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Map the computer, bash, and text editor tools needed for a desktop automation agent. Include validation rules for each action type.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Deployment Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this computer-use agent deployment plan for unsafe screen capture, command execution, credential access, and missing human approvals.

output_examples.input: Design a safe environment for a GUI agent that browses internal tools.
output_examples.output: The response should recommend a virtual desktop, restricted network routes, no host credential mounts, scoped temporary storage, resource limits, and audit logging.

output_examples.input: Review my desktop action loop for unsafe behavior.
output_examples.output: The response should identify missing step limits, unsafe direct host access, unprotected screenshots, weak command controls, and actions that need human approval.

output_examples.input: Compare full desktop control with browser-only automation.
output_examples.output: The response should explain capability differences, operational risk, testing needs, and when a narrower browser automation tool is safer.

best_practices: Run computer-use agents only inside isolated virtual desktops or containers.
Limit commands, network access, storage paths, credentials, runtime, and action count.
Log screenshots and actions carefully, and redact sensitive data when possible.
anti_patterns: Do not run a computer-use agent directly on a primary workstation.
Do not expose unrestricted bash or filesystem access to model-generated actions.
Do not store screenshots or action logs where unrelated users can read them.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design agents that observe screens and control desktop interfaces through mouse, keyboard, shell, and editor tools.

faq.question: Is this a complete working agent?
faq.answer: No. It is guidance with partial implementation examples and security patterns.

faq.question: Why is sandboxing required?
faq.answer: Screen control and command execution can affect real systems, expose data, or make unintended changes.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The concepts can guide Claude Code workflows, but tool availability and permissions still need local configuration.

faq.question: Does it cover OpenAI CUA?
faq.answer: Yes. The description includes OpenAI Operator and CUA concepts alongside Anthropic Computer Use and open-source approaches.

faq.question: What should I verify before production use?
faq.answer: Verify model versions, tool APIs, command allowlists, container isolation, credential handling, logging, and human approval points.
